infons.title: Subject areas

passages.text: ADSPAO: Enhanced artemisinin optimization for multi-threshold segmentation of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease
Summary

Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) poses a significant threat to global public health, with its diagnosis heavily reliant on accurate segmentation of medical computed tomography (CT) images. However, current segmentation methods suffer from limited optimization efficiency, accuracy, and high computational costs. To address these issues, this paper proposes an improved artemisinin optimization algorithm (ADSPAO), which incorporates spiral propagation (SP) and adaptive delivery (AD) strategies to enhance local exploitation and global exploration, respectively. Experimental comparisons on the IEEE CEC2017 benchmark show that ADSPAO exhibits superior robustness and stability across various function types. Furthermore, the algorithm is applied to multi-threshold segmentation of COPD CT images using Renyi entropy as the fitness function. Results demonstrate that ADSPAO achieves better segmentation performance in terms of peak signal to noise ratio (PSNR), structural similarity (SSIM), and feature similarity (FSIM) compared to other methods, offering an efficient and reliable solution for medical image segmentation.
Graphical abstract
Highlights
Proposes an improved artemisinin optimization algorithm (ADSPAO)
Introduces adaptive delivery and spiral migration to enhance performance
Achieves strong stability, robustness, and accuracy on CEC2017 benchmarks
Significantly improves accuracy, stability, and robustness in COPD CT segmentation
Health sciences; Medicine; Health informatics
Introduction

Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) is a progressive lung disease with common symptoms including dyspnea, chronic cough and airflow obstruction, which seriously affects the sufferers’ quality of life. The diagnosis of COPD mainly relies on pulmonary function tests, image analysis, and other methods, but due to its complex and diverse symptoms, it is hard to accurately identify the lesion area on the pathology image, which brings many challenges to diagnosis. In this context, image segmentation (IS) technology plays a crucial role in the diagnosis of COPD, which can effectively assist doctors in identifying and quantifying the lesion area of the lung, thus increasing the efficacy and accuracy of diagnosis.

IS, as an important image processing technology, is dedicated to segmenting images into multiple regions with different characteristics. It has been widely used in the medical field and can segment lesion areas in computed tomography (CT), MRI, and other images to assist doctors in disease analysis. With the rapid development of artificial intelligence technology, new IS methods continue to emerge, providing more efficient and accurate solutions for the medical field. For instance, Yan et al. suggested a novel diffusion model that treats medical image segmentation (MIS) as a denoising problem and improves the ability to focus on the target area through contrast enhancement and conditional cross-attention modules. The model outperforms mainstream methods on multiple datasets, verifying its potential for segmentation accuracy and efficiency. Shen et al. proposed a dual-encoder segmentation network based on Transformer and HarDNet. They extracted local features through HarDNet and obtained global features through Transformer, and integrated multi-dimensional feature information, which improved the segmentation accuracy and performed well on multiple datasets. Qian et al. offered an automated segmentation approach for medical ultrasound images based on an improved fully convolutional network (FCN). Through image preprocessing, multi-scale dilated convolution to capture contextual features, and Laplace correction operator post-processing, they achieved accurate edge segmentation, improved segmentation accuracy and shortened segmentation time. Joshi et al. suggested a new MIS model that mixes the level set evolution model, local gradient flow image, and saliency analysis to perform brain tumor segmentation in 2019 through quantitative and qualitative analysis dataset, demonstrating the superiority of this model in terms of precision, robustness, and computational efficiency compared to existing techniques. Qi et al. proposed the coordinate distillation method to train the student network to imitate the teacher network by integrating channel and spatial information to improve the accuracy of brain tumor segmentation. Experiments show that this method significantly improves segmentation accuracy while maintaining efficiency.

To address the issue of SAM’s poor performance in MIS due to a lack of domain knowledge, Wu et al. proposed the edical SAM adapter (Med-SA), which updates only 2% of its parameters. This adapter extends 2D SAM to 3D medical images via SD-Trans and introduces HyP-Adpt to achieve cue-driven adaptive adjustment. In 17 cross-modal tasks, it outperforms fully fine-tuned SAM and various specialized models, verifying the efficiency and superiority of the lightweight adaptation strategy in medical IS. To address the issues of vision Mamba’s tendency to introduce redundant information through global scanning and its insensitivity to local details in MIS, Wu et al. proposed a higher-order vision Mamba UNet (H-vmunet). H-SS2D is used to progressively suppress redundancy, and Local-SS2D is used to enhance local details, embedding both into a U-shaped jump connection framework. On three public datasets, H-vmunet reduces the number of parameters and computational cost by 67% and 82%, respectively, compared to VM-UNet, while significantly improving DSC, validating the efficiency and superiority of higher-order state-space models in medical image segmentation.

IS techniques can generally be divided into edge-based segmentation, region-based segmentation, clustering-based segmentation, deep learning-based segmentation, threshold-based segmentation and other methods. Among them, edge-based segmentation depends on the clarity of the edge in the image and has poor effect on medical images with more noise interference or blurred boundaries; region-based segmentation methods are prone to over-segmentation or under-segmentation of regions; clustering-based segmentation methods usually rely on the initial conditions and parameter settings of the data and have limited effect on class distinction in complex scenes; although deep learning-based segmentation methods have higher accuracy, they have high computational cost and high requirements for computing resources and are not easy to be extended to resource-constrained application scenarios. In contrast, threshold-based segmentation methods are simple to implement, highly efficient, and require only a small number of parameters and computing resources, which can show great advantages in MIS.

The working mechanism of multi-threshold image segmentation (MTIS) is to separate the grayscale level of the image into several intervals by selecting several thresholds, so as to segment the image into multiple regions with similar features. Specifically, several thresholds are automatically or manually selected, which divide the pixel grayscale range of the image into multiple levels, and each pixel is assigned to a specific area according to its grayscale value. In this way, pixels with similar brightness or similar features are gathered in the same area, thereby dividing the image into information blocks with different characteristics, which is convenient for subsequent analysis and processing. The multi-threshold segmentation method is suitable for complex scenes containing multiple grayscale levels in the image; it is therefore frequently utilized in medical image processing. Among the related technologies of MTIS, meta-heuristic algorithms (MAs) have gradually become an important tool in MTIS due to their high efficiency and robustness in complex optimization problems. Compared with traditional methods, MAs achieve global search by simulating group behaviors in nature (such as evolution, predation, etc.), can effectively avoid falling into local optimality, have high robustness and adaptability, and have significant advantages in multi-threshold segmentation.

MAs are a type of methods that simulate social or natural processes to solve challenging optimization issues. Their core goal is to search the approximate optimal solution to the optimization problem through global search. MAs usually don’t depend on the particulars of the issue, so they can be applied to different fields. This type of algorithm mainly includes two strategies: global exploration and local development. Global exploration can support the algorithm search diverse solutions in the solution space and avoid tumbling into the local optimum; local development further refines after finding a high-quality solution to approach the optimal solution. Four broad categories can be used to classify common MAs.

The first type is based on evolutionary algorithms, which simulate the biological evolution process in nature and gradually optimize the solution through operations, such as individual selection, crossover, and mutation in the population to approach the global optimum. This type of algorithm uses the principle of “survival of the fittest” to continuously enhance the overall quality of the population, so it is suitable for multi-objective optimization and complex search space. Typical evolutionary algorithms include: genetic algorithm (GA), differential evolution (DE), etc.

The second type is algorithms based on group behavior, which improves search efficiency and accuracy by sharing information between individuals. This type of algorithm usually optimizes the search path through individual collaboration and competition, and is particularly suitable for global search problems. Representative algorithms include: ant colony optimization (ACO), gray wolf optimizer (GWO), social cognitive optimization (SCO), firefly algorithm (FA), moth-flame optimization (MFO), particle swarm optimization (PSO), Harris hawks optimization (HHO), hunger games search (HGS), artificial bee colony algorithm (ABC), cuckoo search (CS), bat algorithm (BA), whale optimization algorithm (WOA), parrot optimizer (PO), liver cancer algorithm (LCA), artemisinin optimization (AO), slime mold algorithm (SMA), and colony predation algorithm (CPA), among others.

The third type is algorithms based on mathematical rules, which rely on mathematical models and mathematical rules to guide the search path and usually have good convergence performance and efficient search strategies. This type of algorithm uses mathematical tools such as randomness and probability to make the algorithm more adaptable and robust. Common algorithms include: sine cosine algorithm (SCA), weighted mean of vectors (INFO), and Runge Kutta Optimizer (RUN), etc.

The last type is algorithms based on natural phenomena, which simulate physical or chemical ecological phenomena in nature, such as chemical reactions, ecological competition, etc., and seek optimal solutions through interactions between individuals or gradual changes in physical phenomena. This type of algorithm uses the balance and iterative mechanism of natural phenomena and is suitable for complex multi-peak optimization problems. Common algorithms include: biogeography-based optimization (BBO), RIME optimization algorithm (RIME), simulated annealing (SA), gravitational search algorithm (GSA), etc.

With its global search and strong adaptability, MAs have been widely used in various fields, including engineering design problems, energy optimization, machine learning, feature selection, pattern recognition, image processing, etc. However, according to the “no free lunch theorem”, no algorithm can perform best on all problems. Different optimization problems require specific algorithm adjustments, so researchers have made various improvements to MAs to better adapt to different application scenarios. In recent years, researchers have proposed optimization methods that integrate multiple strategies and mix different algorithms and have achieved remarkable results. For instance, Su et al. suggested a new method named CCFCS to solve the optimization performance in engineering problems. The algorithm enhances the search capacity and ability to leave local optimality of the traditional CS algorithm by combining the cross optimizer and the decentralized foraging strategy. Aghabeigi et al. proposed an efficient facial emotion recognition system based on convolutional neural network (CNN) and local sorted binary pattern (LSBP) to address the challenge of feature extraction in automatic facial emotion recognition and used the WOA to optimize the hyperparameters of CNN to achieve a high-performance facial emotion recognition model. Peng et al. proposed a WOA that combines information sharing search mechanism and Nelder-Mead simplex mechanism, namely ISNMWOA, to enhance the accuracy and efficiency of photovoltaic model parameter estimation. The algorithm obtains the initial vector of photovoltaic model parameters through global search, then uses information sharing strategy to perform coarse local search, and finally performs fine local search through Nelder-Mead simplex mechanism to obtain the optimal model parameter vector, which has achieved good results in parameter estimation tasks. Samieiyan et al. introduced a crow search algorithm to address the shortcomings of existing algorithms in feature selection problems, such as insufficient feature reduction and easy to fall into local optimal solutions. The researchers introduced dynamic perception probability to balance the local and global search processes and suggested a new neighborhood assignment strategy to optimize local search. The algorithm has good performance in feature selection tasks. Zhang et al. improved an MMG feature selection method based on the chameleon swarm algorithm (CSA) and the grasshopper optimization algorithm (GOA) to identify the knee and ankle joint motion patterns in sitting and standing postures. The study designed a wireless multi-channel MMG acquisition system, collected MMG signals from four parts of the thigh and analyzed the relationship between the threshold and classification accuracy.

Although MAs have significant advantages in solving complex optimization problems, they still have some disadvantages. First, due to its reliance on random search and probabilistic operations, the algorithm usually converges slowly, and the quality and stability of the solution fluctuate greatly between different runs. In addition, MAs are sensitive to parameter settings, and improper parameter selection may lead to performance degradation. Moreover, during the search process, the balance between global and local search is difficult to grasp, which can easily cause the algorithm to tumble into a local optimum or be difficult to converge to the optimal solution. At the same time, MAs are difficult to apply universally to a variety of problems, and perform particularly poorly in high-dimensional and complex problems. They usually require specific adjustments or mixing with other algorithms to improve the effect.

The AO is a newly suggested nature-inspired optimization algorithm that simulates the process of artemisinin eradicating malaria in the human body and gets a balance between local exploitation and global exploration through a staged treatment mechanism. The original AO algorithm performs well in global search capabilities and multi-peak problems but has low convergence efficiency in high-dimensional problems, insufficient local development accuracy, and limited population diversity maintenance capabilities. To deal with these shortcomings, this study proposes an enhanced AO method (ADSPAO), which introduces an adaptive delivery mechanism and a spiral propagation operator to dynamically adjust the search strategy and optimization path, further achieving a balance between exploration and exploitation, improving the algorithm’s accuracy and speed of convergence while enhancing its local and global search capabilities.

In addition, we carried out comprehensive comparative experiments in the IEEE CEC2017 benchmark to confirm ADSPAO’s exceptional performance, including the performance with the original AO and other advanced optimization methods on unimodal functions, multimodal functions, hybrid functions, and composition functions. According to experimental findings, ADSPAO offers significant advantages in global search capabilities, convergence accuracy, and population diversity maintenance, especially in multimodal and combination functions, showing excellent optimization effects.

Finally, in order to resolve the IS issue of COPD, this study proposes an MTIS method based on ADSPAO, which uses Renyi entropy as the objective function to automatically select the ideal threshold to segment the lesion area in medical CT images. The segmentation effect is evaluated by three metrics: peak signal to noise ratio (PSNR), structural similarity (SSIM), and feature similarity (FSIM), and the ranking results, such as average value (Avg), standard deviation (SD), and Wilcoxon signed-rank test (WSRT) are combined for analysis. We tested the suggested approach against some competing algorithms on a variety of test images to confirm its efficacy. The experimental findings clearly show how much better ADSPAO is at the MTIS task.

An improved artemisinin optimization method (ADSPAO) is proposed. In view of the shortcomings of the original AO algorithm in convergence speed, stability, and high-dimensional optimization problems, this paper introduces an adaptive delivery mechanism and a spiral migration operator, which greatly improves the algorithm’s capacity for global search and local exploitation accuracy.
The improved algorithm demonstrates excellent stability, robustness, and convergence accuracy in benchmark tests.
The proposed algorithm was applied to the COPD medical CT image segmentation task, which significantly improved the segmentation accuracy, stability, and robustness.

This study used a COPD medical image dataset from hospital, to verify the efficacy of the proposed ADSPAO-based Renyi entropy MTIS method on actual clinical data, successfully solving practical problems in medical image segmentation.
The following are this paper’s primary contributions.
Results
Experiments on the benchmark functions

In order to assess the optimization capability of the proposed ADSPAO algorithm, a series of experiments were conducted in this section. First, an ablation experiment was carried out to analyze the contribution of each module to the overall capability by removing or replacing different components of the ADSPAO; secondly, a balanced diversity analysis was conducted to evaluate the balance between global exploration and local development of ADSPAO and ensure that the algorithm has sufficient search diversity; further, a stability analysis was conducted to compare with the original AO algorithm in 10, 30, 50, and 100 dimensions, respectively, to check the optimization effect and stability of ADSPAO in different dimensions; finally, ADSPAO was compared with the original algorithm and other advanced algorithms at IEEE CEC2017, respectively. To ensure the advantage and stability of the ADSPAO’s performance, the Avg and SD) were used to analyze the experimental results, and the best results were marked in bold. By observing the smaller Avg value, the degree of closeness of the algorithm to the optimal solution of the benchmark functions can be judged; moreover, the smaller SD indicates the stability and reliability of the algorithm. In addition, the results of WSRT and Friedman test (FT) were used to further analyze the experiment, verifying the superior performance of ADSPAO and its competitive advantage under multiple benchmarks. The significance level of WSRT is set to 0.05. If the p value produced by the comparison is less than 0.05, it indicates that ADSPAO’s efficiency has significantly improved compared with the optimizer; on the contrary, it means that the performance gap between ADSPAO and the comparison algorithm is nearly insignificant. The symbol “+” indicates that ADSPAO outperforms the compared algorithms; the symbol “−“ indicates that the performance of ADSPAO is inferior to the other algorithms; the symbol “ = ” indicates that the performance is equivalent. In addition, the convergence comparison diagram of the experiment is given and analyzed to facilitate a more intuitive understanding and analysis of the experimental findings.

To guarantee the reliability and fairness of the experiment, the experiments in this section were conducted under the same hardware conditions, all implemented on the same Intel i7-12700 (2.10 GHz) and 32 GB memory under Windows 11 and MATLAB 2020a software platforms. At the same time, this study uses the number of evaluations instead of the number of iterations and sets the maximum number of evaluations to 300,000. In addition, the population size is set to 30, the problem dimension is 30, and all algorithms undergo 30 independent tests to reduce the influence of random factors. The experiment uses 29 benchmark functions in IEEE CEC2017 for testing. In IEEE CEC2017, F1 and F3 are unimodal functions, F4-F10 are multimodal functions, F11-F20 are hybrid functions, and F21-F30 are composition functions. Other specific details about IEEE CEC2017 can be seen in Table S1. In addition, the exact parameter settings of the competing algorithms utilized in the experiment are provided in Table 1.
Ablation experiment
Strategy comparison description
AD	SP	 	ADSPAO	yes	yes	 	ADAO	yes	no	 	SPAO	no	yes	 	AO	no	no

The main purpose of the ablation experiment is to check the efficacy of introducing the AD and SP strategy by comparing different variants of the ADSPAO algorithm. By comparing various variants of the algorithm, we can more clearly understand the specific contribution of each strategy to the algorithm performance. For detailed information on the variants, see Table 2, where “yes” means that the strategy is adopted, and “no” means that the strategy is not adopted.
Various AO variants with two strategies
1Bold values indicate the best performance among all compared methods.

Table 3 shows the results of WSRT. Through observation, we can see that with an average ranking of 1.4828, ADSPAO is in first place, proving its comprehensive capabilities in global search and local development. ADAO and SPAO ranked second and third with rankings of 2.1724 and 2.5862, respectively, indicating that introducing AD or SP strategies alone can improve some performance, but the effect is not as good as the complete ADSPAO algorithm. AO ranks fourth with an average ranking of 3.7586, further verifying the efficiency deficiencies of the original AO. In addition, Table S2 shows the detailed p value results of WSRT, indicating that there are significant differences between ADSPAO and other variants, further proving the effectiveness of the introduction strategy.
The Friedman ranking of ADSPAO, ADAO, SPAO, and AO

Figure 1 shows the results of FT. ADSPAO is also ahead of other variants with an average ranking of 1.91, which more intuitively demonstrates the significant improvement of the algorithm after the introduction of two strategies.
Convergence curves of the algorithm for each mechanism of the ablation experiments

Figure 2 shows the convergence graphs of three different variants compared with the original AO, which further verifies the superiority of ADSPAO and shows that the introduced adaptive transfer and spiral propagation strategies can successfully enhance the algorithm’s capacity for global search and eventually converge to a better solution. However, the experiment also shows that after the introduction of these two strategies, although the final convergence accuracy of the ADSPAO is enhanced, the convergence speed is sacrificed in the early phase of optimization. This means that ADSPAO is more cautious in early exploration, giving priority to ensuring global search capabilities, although it can better develop local areas in the later stage to obtain better solutions.

Such a slower early convergence may have a dual effect in practical applications such as MIS. On one hand, it increases computational time during the initial optimization stage; on the other hand, it prevents premature convergence and allows the algorithm to explore a wider range of threshold combinations, which is crucial for achieving higher segmentation precision. In particular, for MTIS tasks, maintaining sufficient global diversity in the early iterations helps the algorithm avoid overfitting to local gray-level distributions and improves the overall structural similarity and feature preservation of the segmented images.

Although the ablation results confirm the effectiveness of the AD and SP strategies, their specific performance differences across different categories of functions reveal their complementary roles in the optimization process. For unimodal functions, the search scenario is relatively simple. In this case, the SP strategy enhances fine-grained exploitation by generating spiral convergence behavior around promising regions, thus making a greater contribution. However, since unimodal functions require less exploration, the contribution of AD is relatively limited. Therefore, ADSPAO does not exhibit a distinct advantage on unimodal functions.

For multimodal, hybrid, and composition functions, the functions contain many local optima and deceptive basins. The AD mechanism dynamically adjusts the exploration intensity according to population diversity, enabling the algorithm to escape local optima and maintain the effectiveness of global search. Meanwhile, once a promising region is identified, the SP strategy gradually refines the search to improve solution accuracy. Therefore, AD improves the breadth of the search, whereas SP improves the manner of searching within promising regions. This leads ADSPAO to converge more cautiously in the early stage to ensure exploration, and to converge faster in the later stage due to SP-driven exploitation. Thus, the superior performance of ADSPAO arises not only from introducing the two strategies but also from their dynamic balance between exploration and exploitation.
Stability experiment

The stability of the algorithm plays a vital role in optimization problems. An algorithm with good stability can always maintain a relatively consistent optimization effect under different dimensions and test conditions, avoiding large fluctuations in results due to different specific application conditions. Therefore, stability is one of the key metrics to assess the practicality of an optimization algorithm.
Mean and standard deviation of ADSPAO and AO on different dimensions
1Bold values indicate the best results.

The main purpose of this experiment is to evaluate the stability of the improved ADSPAO in different dimensions and verify whether it can stably obtain the global optimal solution when dealing with low-dimensional and high-dimensional problems. The experiment set up four optimization tasks with different dimensions of 10, 30, 50, and 100, and compared the results of ADSPAO with the original AO algorithm. Table 4 displays the outcomes of the experiment.

Experimental results show that ADSPAO with the introduction of AD and SP significantly enhances the global search capabilities and local development capabilities. When solving optimization problems, ADSPAO has greatly enhanced stability compared to the original AO and can search the optimal solution to the problem quicker and more precise. In tests of different dimensions, ADSPAO performs better than the original AO algorithm, especially the optimization effect on low-dimensional and medium-dimensional problems is more prominent. For example, when the dimension is 10, on F3, the Avg is 3.0000E + 02 and the SD is 4.8372E − 14, which is close to 0, indicating that the results are highly concentrated and stable. In addition, ADSPAO also achieved good performance in high-dimensional complex functions. For example, when dim = 100, the Avg value of ADSPAO on F6 is 6.0002E + 02, and the SD value is 1.0878E − 02, which reflects good optimization performance and result stability, proving its advantages in solving high-dimensional complex problems.
WSRT of stability experiment

Table 5 displays the WSRT rankings of ADSPAO and AO on 29 benchmark functions in different dimensions. In the four dimensions of 10, 30, 50, and 100, the comprehensive ranking of ADSPAO surpasses the original AO algorithm.
Convergence curves of stability experiment when Dim = 10, 30, 50, 100

In order to more intuitively show the progress of ADSPAO, Figure 3 provides the stability test convergence curves of the two algorithms in 10, 30, 50, and 100 dimensions. Among them, we selected the convergence graphs of three test functions, namely the unimodal function F1, the hybrid function F13, and the composition function F30, in each dimension. Obviously, the convergence accuracy of ADSPAO in each figure is far better than that of the AO algorithm, and a better solution can be found in specific problems.
Comparison with original MAs
With the aim to confirm the algorithm’s optimization performance, this part compares ADSPAO with 9 basic algorithms, including DE, HHO, PSO, GWO, WOA, SMA, SCA, MFO, and RUN.
Comparison of ADSPAO with original algorithm at IEEE CEC2017
1Bold values indicate the best results.

Table 6 displays the Avg and SD values of ADSPAO and the 9 original algorithms after 30 independent runs. In the comparative experiments with the original algorithms, there are certain differences in the performance of ADSPAO on different types of functions. Specifically, ADSPAO’s performance on unimodal functions is relatively poor, which shows that its development capabilities in dealing with simple optimization problems still need to be strengthened. However, in multimodal functions, hybrid functions, and combinatorial functions, ADSPAO shows obvious advantages and can obtain the best solution in most test functions, especially in global search and jumping out of local optimality. In the tests of these complex functions, ADSPAO not only achieved the first place in multiple test functions but also ranked relatively high in the remaining functions and was able to enter the top three. However, regarding the stability analysis, based on the performance of the SD results, it is evident that ADSPAO’s performance is not as expected, and its stability shows large fluctuations in some tests. This shows that although ADSPAO has certain advantages in finding high-quality solutions, the stability and consistency of its solutions still need to be improved in multiple runs. Future improvements can consider introducing more stability optimization mechanisms to increase the reliability of the method on different test functions.
Results of ADSPAO with the original algorithm on WSRT
1Bold values indicate the best performance among all compared methods.
The Friedman ranking of ADSPAO and original algorithms

Table 7 shows the ranking results obtained by WSRT. In the WSRT comparison with the original algorithm, ADSPAO achieved first place with an average ranking of 1.7241, significantly ahead of the second-ranked DE algorithm (average ranking 3.0345). This result shows that ADSPAO surpasses other original algorithms in overall performance. Especially on most test functions, ADSPAO performs better than other algorithms, demonstrating its broad adaptability in diverse problem environments. Table S3 displays the p value results of ADSPAO compared with each algorithm. A detailed analysis of the p value table shows that the p values of ADSPAO on most functions are less than 0.05, which means that the difference with other algorithms is statistically significant. In short, ADSPAO not only performs well in terms of convergence speed and accuracy but also demonstrates stronger global exploration capabilities and robustness. In complex functions and multimodal functions, ADSPAO can find the global optimal solution more effectively and shows significant optimization performance advantages in comparison with other algorithms. In addition, Figure 4 provides the Friedman average level and overall ranking of ADSPAO and all original comparison algorithms, which can more intuitively observe the leading position of ADSPAO.
Convergence curves of the ADSPAO algorithm and the original algorithm at IEEE CEC2017

With the aim of understanding the convergence of the methods more easily, Figure 5 displays the convergence curves of ADSPAO and other basic algorithms and selected the convergence diagrams of six functions for comparative analysis to demonstrate ADSPAO performance in comparative experiments with the original algorithm. These convergence diagrams show that ADSPAO exhibits excellent convergence accuracy on all test functions and can obtain higher-quality solutions, reflecting the algorithm’s significant advantages in optimization capabilities. In addition, ADSPAO has excellent convergence speed on some functions and can reach convergence quickly, demonstrating its potential to accelerate convergence in certain scenarios. However, in some functions, although the convergence accuracy is still better than other original algorithms, its convergence speed is not the fastest. This shows that ADSPAO has certain limitations in the speed balance between the development and exploration phases. Therefore, the convergence curve results of ADSPAO not only demonstrate its advantages in improving the accuracy of the solution but also reveal its shortcomings in improving the overall convergence speed. These results reflect ADSPAO’s strengths in optimization accuracy and some room for improvement in optimization speed.
Comparison with advanced MAs

With the aim of further demonstrating the advantages of the ADSPAO suggested in this study, this section selects 12 enhanced MAs and compares these algorithms with ADSPAO, including: LSHADE_cnEpSi, CLPSO, ALCPSO, CGPSO, OBSCA, LGWO, ISNMWOA, BLRIME, LACOR, XMACOR, IWOA, and RCBA.
Comparison of ADSPAO with advanced algorithms at IEEE CEC2017
1Bold values indicate the best results.

As shown in Table 8, in the comparative experiments with advanced algorithms, ADSPAO has excellent overall performance on multi-modal functions and combination functions, especially achieving minimum values in multiple test functions, demonstrating its excellent global search capability and strong adaptability, which can effectively prevent being trapped in local optima and quickly find the global optimal solution.
The Friedman ranking of ADSPAO and advanced algorithms

However, the experimental results also reveal some shortcomings. Although ADSPAO performs well on most functions, it is slightly insufficient when dealing with unimodal functions and fails to achieve the expected optimal results. This shows that ADSPAO still needs to improve its local development capabilities, especially when performing detailed searches on unimodal problems, its stability and accuracy are still lacking. This limitation can be mainly attributed to the design of the AD and SP strategies. Specifically, the AD mechanism tends to emphasize global exploration by dynamically adjusting population diversity, which may lead to excessive search dispersion in simple unimodal landscapes. Meanwhile, the SP strategy, though designed to enhance local exploitation, is activated mainly in the later stages of optimization, resulting in insufficient fine-tuning accuracy when the search space contains only one global optimum. Consequently, the algorithm may take longer to converge to the precise minimum. Therefore, although ADSPAO performs strongly in global search, its room for improvement in local development and stability still needs further exploration. Figure 6 shows the comparison results of FT, further intuitively demonstrating the superiority of ADSPAO.
Results of ADSPAO with other advanced algorithm on WSRT
1Bold values indicate the best performance among all compared methods.

Table 9 displays the WSRT results of ADSPAO and other advanced algorithms. Among all algorithms, ADSPAO ranks first with an average ranking of 3.0690, which is better than other advanced algorithms, indicating the advantages of ADSPAO in global and local search. At the same time, the data in Table S4 further supports this conclusion. Most of the p svalues in the table are less than 0.05, demonstrating that ADSPAO is superior to other algorithms in a statistically meaningful way. This means that ADSPAO has higher reliability and stability in optimization results than other algorithms, which fully proves its excellent performance on different test functions.
Convergence curves of the ADSPAO algorithm and advanced algorithms at IEEE CEC2017

Figure 7 exhibits the convergence curves of ADSPAO on some functions, including multimodal functions, hybrid functions and composition functions. Through these convergence graphs, the optimization effect of ADSPAO can be more intuitively demonstrated. The outcomes of the experiment demonstrate that ADSPAO has excellent convergence accuracy on these functions, and all selected convergence graphs show their optimal convergence accuracy, demonstrating that the quality of the final solution produced by ADSPAO is higher than that of other advanced algorithms. Of course, the algorithm also has certain shortcomings, such as slow convergence speed in the early phase. This reveals that although ADSPAO has advantages in global search abilities and final results, improving the convergence speed in the early stage of optimization is still an important improvement direction in the future.
Experiments on MTIS
Experiment setup and database
COPD CT test images and their corresponding 2D histograms
Each panel (A–I) displays a test CT image on the left and its 2D histogram on the right.

The only criterion for determining truth is practice. In this section, we apply the proposed ADSPAO method to MTIS to demonstrate its efficacy in real clinical applications. The images used in this study are from COPD patients in the First Affiliated Hospital of Wenzhou Medical University, China. We randomly selected 9 pathological images for experiments, named A, B, C, D, E, F, G, H, and I, to verify the performance of the algorithm. The selected images and their histograms are shown in Figure 8. To guarantee the fairness of the experiment, all compared methods were evaluated under the equal conditions. The maximum number of evaluations was set to 2,000 times, the image size was set to 512 × 512, the population size was set to 20, and all algorithms were tested independently 30 times. In addition, in order to accurately evaluate the performance of each algorithm at different IS threshold levels, we set the threshold levels to 10, 15, 20, 25, and 30. The compared algorithms involved include: WOA, CGPSO, MGSMA, MDE, IGWO, and GLSMA. This study has been granted ethical approval by the Institutional Review Board of the First Affiliated Hospital of Wenzhou Medical University. As this is a retrospective study, written informed consent was waived by the Institutional Review Board.
Performance evaluation metrics

This paper uses three image quality evaluation indicators, namely PSNR, SSIM, and FSIM to evaluate the quality of the segmented image. The best solution is the evaluation result with the highest value.

To assess how much the segmented image differs from the original image, PSNR is utilized, and the expression is shown in Equation 1. The larger the PSNR value, the smaller the distortion.where RMSE refers to the root mean square error, which is defined as shown in Equation 2. Iij refers the pixel grayscale value of the original image, and Segij is the pixel grayscale value of the segmented image. M × N is the image size.

Another often used objective assessment metric is SSIM, which is primarily used to assess an image’s structural integrity. Let I be the unsegmented image and S be the segmented image. The calculation is as follows:where μI and μS refer the average grayscale of images I and S, respectively.  and  represent to the variance of images I and S, respectively. σI,S represents the covariance of images I and S. c1 and c2 are two constants used to maintain the stability of the system.

The FSIM between the segmented image and the original image is reflected by FSIM and is a variant of SSIM. It uses phase congruence (PC) to describe the structure in the image and gradient magnitude (GM) to explain the contrast distortion of the image. The range of FSIM is [0,1]. The segmented image is closer to the test image the bigger the value. The definition of FSIM is displayed in Equations 4, 5, 6, and 7.where Ω refers to the pixel domain of the test image. SL represents the similarity. PCm(X) represents the phase consistency measure. T1 and T2 are constants. E(X) is the response vector magnitude in location X and scale n. ϵ is a very small number to avoid the denominator being equal to 0. An(X) is the amplitude at location X on scale n.
Experiment analyses at different threshold levels

To assess ADSPAO’s IS performance at different threshold levels, this experiment selected 9 images from the picture set as segmented images and the image size was uniformly set to 512 × 512. ADSPAO and other methods were tested for image segmentation at threshold levels of 10, 15, 20, 25, and 30, and the PSNR, SSIM, and FSIM were used to assess the segmentation findings. At the same time, the Avg, SD, WSRT, and other results of the experiment will be further analyzed to verify the effectiveness of ADSPAO.

Table 10, Table 11, Table 12 exhibit the WSRT results of PSNR, SSIM, and FSIM, respectively. Among them, “thresholds” represents the threshold level, “mean” represents the average of the overall ranking, and “rank” represents the ranking level. As evidenced by the table results, the threshold sets obtained using ADSPAO exhibit excellent performance on most images and evaluation metrics across all threshold levels. ADSPAO has the smallest overall ranking average and always ranks first, which fully demonstrates its significant advantages in threshold-based image segmentation tasks.

Tables S5, S6, S7, S8, and S9 in the Appendix shows in detail the average PSNR, SSIM, and FSIM indicators obtained by all methods at different threshold levels, as well as their SD and ranking results. Obviously, ADSPAO has achieved the smallest Avg value on most images and evaluation indicators, accompanied by a small SD value, which fully demonstrates its excellent optimization performance and good stability. Specifically, a higher PSNR value means that ADSPAO retains more details and less noise when segmenting the image, and the image quality is well maintained; a higher SSIM value indicates that the image segmented by ADSPAO has a high structural similarity with the original image and can retain important structural information; and a higher FSIM value reveals that the segmented image is highly consistent with the original image at the feature level, indicating that ADSPAO retains the key features of the image. In addition, as the threshold level continues to increase, the gap between ADSPAO and other algorithms gradually widens, showing an increasingly obvious advantage. In all images and evaluation indicators, ADSPAO ranks first, showing strong adaptability and superiority under different thresholds. At the same time, as the threshold level increases, the values of the three indicators also show an upward trend, which shows that ADSPAO can better adapt to complex IS tasks at high threshold levels, further improving segmentation accuracy and image quality.

Convergence curves of all competitive algorithms with threshold = 30

Figure 9 shows the convergence curves of all compared methods on 9 images when the threshold is 20. The convergence curves at the other threshold levels (10, 15, 25, and 30) are in Figures 10, 11, 12, and 13. According to the convergence curves of each threshold level in the MTIS experiment, it is evident that ADSPAO has achieved the best convergence accuracy in the convergence graphs at all threshold levels. Although the convergence speed of ADSPAO is not the fastest among all algorithms, it has a good balance between convergence speed and convergence accuracy. In the experiment, as the threshold gradually increases, the convergence speed in the early phase is significantly accelerated, showing a good global search ability. However, as the threshold level is further increased, the fitness value gap between ADSPAO and other comparison algorithms gradually narrows, indicating that the convergence difficulty of the algorithm increases under high thresholds. In general, ADSPAO demonstrates excellent convergence accuracy and robust global search performance in MTIS tasks and is able to maintain good optimization effects at higher threshold levels while balancing convergence speed and accuracy.
Segmented results of all competitive algorithms with threshold = 20 on the image

Figure 14 displays the segmentation result of image A and the corresponding threshold result image when the threshold is 20. Based on the outcomes of the experiment, the segmentation quality of ADSPAO is significantly better than other algorithms, especially in the edge processing of key anatomical parts, with higher accuracy, clear boundaries and natural transitions, especially in areas with similar brightness, which fully demonstrates its powerful optimization ability and adaptability in MTIS problems.
Discussion

In this chapter, we will confer the benefits and drawbacks of the proposed algorithm ADSPAO based on all the experiments conducted in this paper. The experiments cover benchmark function testing, ablation experiment, stability analysis, and actual MIS applications. Through these experiments, we can clearly understand the performance of ADSPAO in different scenarios and its advantages and disadvantages.

First, through ablation experiments, we verified the efficacy of each strategy in the ADSPAO. The main role of ablation experiments is to analyze the contribution of each algorithm component and verify the effect of specific mechanisms on the overall performance of the method. In the experiment, we removed different strategies and tested the variant algorithm. The outcomes of the experiment demonstrated that the introduction of each strategy can successfully enhance the optimization capability of the method, especially in terms of search capabilities and global development capabilities. The contribution is significant, which also verifies the rationality of the overall architecture design of ADSPAO.

Secondly, a stability experiment was conducted to compare ADSPAO with the original AO algorithm on 10, 30, 50, and 100 dimensional benchmark functions. The findings of the experiment indicate that ADSPAO performs more stably in different dimensions and can maintain good optimization accuracy and development capabilities, showing strong global search capabilities and population diversity management capabilities, verifying the adaptability and stability of the algorithm in dealing with complex optimization problems.

Then, we comprehensively compared ADSPAO with other original algorithms and advanced optimization algorithms on the IEEE CEC2017 benchmark function. According to the experimental findings, ADSPAO performs outstandingly in global development and exploration capabilities, especially in effectively maintaining population diversity and avoiding falling into local optimality too early. At the same time, ADSPAO has shown strong adaptability in the optimization of multi-modal functions and combinatorial functions with its excellent exploration capabilities. However, for unimodal functions, ADSPAO exhibits relatively weaker performance. This limitation mainly stems from the intrinsic behavior of the AD and SP strategies. The AD mechanism emphasizes global exploration by maintaining population diversity, which may lead to excessive search dispersion in simple unimodal landscapes. Meanwhile, the SP strategy, although designed to enhance local exploitation, is primarily activated during the later optimization phase, resulting in delayed fine-tuning accuracy when the search space contains only one global optimum. As a result, the algorithm may converge more slowly and show weaker local stability in these cases. Future work could introduce an adaptive phase-switching mechanism to dynamically adjust the balance between AD and SP according to the convergence rate or landscape complexity, thereby improving the precision and stability of local exploitation. In general, although ADSPAO shows superior performance in overall optimization, its local development and convergence stability on simpler problems still require further improvement.

Finally, ADSPAO was applied in the segmentation experiment of COPD medical CT images. The experiment uses ADSPAO as the search method and Renyi entropy as the adaptation function to complete the MTIS task. The segmentation effect is evaluated through three indicators: PSNR, SSIM, and FSIM. Meanwhile, the performance of the method is deeply analyzed by combining analysis methods, such as Avg, SD and WSRT ranking. Experimental results show that ADSPAO has significant advantages in threshold search and can quickly determine high-quality segmentation thresholds. In terms of accuracy, stability, and robustness, ADSPAO also performs significantly better than other comparison algorithms, showing strong global optimization capabilities. However, ADSPAO still has certain limitations at low threshold levels, which are manifested in slow convergence speed and insufficient search effect for optimal fitness values. As the threshold increases, the early convergence speed of ADSPAO gradually accelerates, but in terms of convergence accuracy, the gap with other algorithms gradually narrows. Overall, ADSPAO performs well in IS, but there is still room for improvement in some specific cases.
Limitations of the study

To increase the multi-threshold segmentation accuracy of COPD, this paper proposes a Renyi entropy MTIS method based on ADSPAO. In this method, the AO algorithm was first improved and the ADSPAO algorithm was proposed. In solving optimization problems, the traditional AO algorithm has shortcomings, such as slow convergence speed, prone to getting trapped in local optimality, and imbalance between exploration and exploitation capabilities. To address these issues, this study introduces the AD strategy to enhance the exploration ability of the algorithm so that it can jump out of the local optimum more effectively during the search process; Meanwhile, the SP strategy is used to improve the method’s exploitation capabilities, ensuring that the optimal solution can be approached more accurately in the later convergence process. The organic integration of these two strategies not only optimizes the overall performance of the algorithm but also achieves a better balance between exploration and exploitation. After a series of benchmark testing experiments, ADSPAO has demonstrated significant performance improvements in multiple dimensions and is superior to traditional AO algorithms and other advanced algorithms in terms of convergence accuracy, speed, stability, and global search capabilities. Finally, ADSPAO was successfully used in MTIS experiments on COPD medical CT images, and demonstrated strong segmentation capabilities and adaptability, further verifying its practicability and robustness in practical applications.

Although ADSPAO demonstrated significant advantages in experiments, there is still room for improvement. Future research can consider further enhancing the performance of the algorithm in high-dimensional problems and optimizing its convergence speed in complex search spaces. To mitigate the trade-off between convergence speed and exploration depth, future work may focus on designing adaptive scheduling strategies that dynamically adjust the balance between the AD and SP mechanisms according to the convergence rate or population diversity. This would allow the algorithm to accelerate convergence when diversity decreases while maintaining strong exploratory ability in the early search stage. Such adaptive control can make ADSPAO more efficient and better suited for real-time or large-scale medical image analysis tasks.

In addition, ADSPAO still has extensive potential for expansion in application fields, especially in processing complex medical images, gene optimization, machine learning hyperparameter tuning, and other problems, where its actual performance can be further verified.

Method details
AO
Initialization phase
Comprehensive elimination phase

The initialization phase is the starting point of the AO algorithm. The objective of this phase is to generate initial solutions that will serve as the basis for the subsequent optimization process of the algorithm. As shown in Equation 10, the search agent is initialized by randomly distributing in the solution space.where i = 1, 2, 3 …, N, N is the population size. j = 1, 2, 3, …, D, where D is the variable dimension. LBj and UBj represent the minimum and maximum bounds in the jth dimension, respectively. rand is used to generate random numbers between 0 and 1.
Local clearance phase

The comprehensive elimination phase is designed to simulate the process in which high-dose drugs spread rapidly and act widely on various parts of the human body during early treatment. In this phase, the AO algorithm conducts global exploration through a large-scale search in order to find potential global optimal solutions in a complex search space. The update rules of the search agent are as follows:Among them,  refers the position of the i-th agent in the j-th dimension of the t-th generation;  represents the current best position; K, as a probability coefficient, will be dynamically adjusted as the optimization process proceeds. Usually, in the early phases of optimization, a larger K value is set to improve the global search, while in the later phases, K can be gradually reduced to turn the algorithm to local fine optimization. This dynamic adjustment can improve the convergence speed and accuracy of the algorithm and avoid tumbling into the local optimal solution; c is a decay index related to drug concentration, which simulates the exponential decay process of drug concentration over time. The decay model of drug concentration is as follows:where C(t) represents the drug concentration at time t; C0 is the initial drug concentration; and k is the decay rate constant.
Post-consolidation phase

The local clearance phase simulates the process in which low-dose drugs continue to act on the remaining pathogens in the later phases of treatment. The goal of this stage is to accurately locate and eliminate the local optimal solution area in the solution domain. The update rule of the search agent in this phase is as follows:where, X1, X2, X3 are three randomly selected search agents; d is a random coefficient between [0.1, 0.6]; Fitnorm(i) is the normalized fitness value of the i-th search individual, indicating the probability of the search individual being selected. This stage aims to further optimize the quality of the solution through local fine search.

The post-consolidation phase aims to deal with the problem that the algorithm may tumble into a local optimal, simulating the risk of disease recurrence that may occur after the treatment is completed. This phase enhances the algorithm’s capacity to jump out of the local optimal by introducing a certain amount of randomness. The update rules of the search agent are as follows:In this stage, the diversity of solutions is enhanced by random selection and replacement of the optimal solution, thus preventing the algorithm from converging to the local optimum too early.
Adaptive delivery mechanism

The original AO algorithm simulated the process of artemisinin eradicating malaria in the human body. In the process of drug treatment, the mechanism of drug action in the body is a dynamic process, among which the diffusion phenomenon is one of the core links of drug transmission and action in the body. The diffusion of drugs can not only transfer active ingredients from high-concentration areas to low-concentration areas, but also achieve precise treatment through step-by-step action. Therefore, this study proposes a corresponding Adaptive Delivery (AD) mechanism and introduces it into the AO algorithm. The strategy dynamically adjusts the migration process of individuals by introducing adaptive perturbations, so that the method can flexibly switch between global exploration and local development at different stages to enhance the efficiency of the AO algorithm. Specifically, the update of individuals can be described by the following formula:

To improve the ability of the AO to find optimal solutions, we added a dynamic perturbation operator so that the perturbation intensity can gradually decay with the iteration process, thereby realizing the gradual conversion between exploration and development. δ is a perturbation amplitude control parameter, which mainly affects the range of drug diffusion and determines the drug diffusion path. The specific calculation is shown in Equation 18. e is a random number in the range of [-1,1], which is used to introduce randomness in the search process. Temp is used to adjust the position of the current individual in some cases, and the formula is shown in Equation 19. The individual Xi of the population needs to be updated by Xi = Xi(pick), while pick = randperm(dim), and p randomly arranges the dimensions to select specific dimensions for perturbation. This random dimension selection increases the diversity of the algorithm, allowing individuals to update different dimensions in different iterations, thereby enhancing the search capability.
Spiral propagation

There are many phenomena in nature that occur in the form of spiral motion. This type of motion has unique advantages in terms of dynamics and balance. It can not only cover a wide space to achieve global search, but also achieve fine local optimization through gradual convergence. To continue to enhance the global search capability of the AO and enhance its local search efficiency, this study intends to introduce a spiral migration operator to update the position of individuals through a spiral path to achieve the purpose of balanced exploration and development. The mathematical modeling of spiral Propagation (SP) is as follows:where, Ei,j is the sorted population; Di,j is the distance between the population and the sorted population, as shown in Equation 21; b is a constant equal to 1, which is used to control the shape of the logarithmic spiral function; k and c are defined as shown in Equation 23 and Equation 24, respectively, and the value of c decreases with the increase of the number of evaluations; cos 2πk represents the angular change part in the spiral path, which makes the individual move along the spiral shape in different dimensions; In Equation 22, round means rounding.

By introducing the SP strategy, the AO algorithm can more effectively balance local convergence and global exploration in the search space, enhancing the performance of the ADSPAO in complex high-dimensional optimization problems.
Modified AO (ADSPAO)
The pseudocode for ADSPAO
Input: UB, LB, FEs, MaxFEs, N, D.
1: Initialize the rime population X;
2: Calculate the fitness values for every individual;
3: Select the best agent Xbest and calculate the optimal fitness bestfitness;
4: while FEs < MaxFEs do.
5: Update the relevant parameters E, K and c;
6: for i = 1 to N do.
7: if rand < K then.
8: Perform the comprehensive elimination operation as shown in Equation 11;
9: end if.
10: Calculate the normalized fitness value Fitnorm(i) of the current individual;
11: if rand < Fitnorm(i) then.
12: Update the current individual position by Equation 14;
13: end if.
14: if rand < 0.2 then.
15: Enter the post-consolidation phase and improve the algorithm’s capability to escape from the local optimum through Equation 16;
16: end if.
17: Sort the population according to the fitness value;
18: Update the population individuals in a spiral propagation manner, as shown in Equation 20;
19: Use the greedy selection mechanism to select the individual and update Xbest and bestfitness;
20: FEs = FEs + 1;
21: end for.
22: for i = 1 to N do.
23: Update δ by Equation 18;
24: Update the population according to Equation 17;
25: FEs = FEs + 1;
26: if F(Xnew) < bestfitness then.
27: Update the best individual Xbest as well as the best fitness value bestfitness.
28: end if.
29: end for.
30: end while.
Output: The best solution Xbest

Since no algorithm can perform best on all problems, even if AO performs well in global search and local development, there are still problems such as low exploration efficiency and simple to tumble into local optimality. This is because during the optimization process, individuals gradually concentrate in some local areas, resulting in a decrease in search diversity and difficulty in jumping out of the local optimal solution. In addition, due to the relatively fixed update mechanism of the AO algorithm and the lack of adaptable global exploration methods, the optimization efficiency may not be satisfactory when dealing with multimodal functions and complex search spaces. Therefore, it is particularly important to enhance the method’s search accuracy and avoid premature convergence. In summary, this study proposed an improved AO algorithm named ADSPAO by introducing the adaptive delivery mechanism and the spiral propagation operator, which achieved a balance between global search and local convergence. On the one hand, the adaptive delivery mechanism dynamically adjusts the individual search to improve the global search capability; on the other hand, the spiral propagation refines the search path, improves the local exploitation performance and expands the search range, thereby accelerating convergence and improving the accuracy of the solution. At the same time, unlike traditional algorithms, the improved ADSPAO in this paper adopts an active greedy selection mechanism, which can improve the accuracy of the solution while accelerating the convergence. The specific flowchart of the proposed ADSPAO is shown in Figure S1, and Algorithm 1 displays the pseudocode execution procedure.
Computational complexity analysis

The computational complexity of the ADSPAO algorithm is mainly affected by factors such as the number of iterations, population size, and dimension size. First, let the population size be N, and the dimension of each individual be dim. In the main loop, let T be the number of iterations obtained by the maximum number of evaluations MaxFEs. Assuming that the algorithm evaluates M times in each iteration, T is represented by MaxFEs/M. Then O(AO) = O((N × dim) × logn × (1 + T)), O(AD) = O(T × N × dim), and O(SP) = O(T × N × dim). Consequently, the total complexity of the ADSPAO is O(ADSPAO) = O(T × N × dim + (N × dim) × logn × (1 + T)).
MTIS

MTIS is a technique that divides an image into multiple non-overlapping regions, each corresponding to a specific grayscale range. The traditional single threshold method can only distinguish between the foreground and the background, while the multi-threshold method can better separate the detailed structure in the image, which is of great significance in medical image processing, especially in tumor recognition. However, choosing a suitable threshold combination is a challenging optimization problem, which directly affects the quality of segmentation. To overcome this problem, various optimization algorithms have been introduced, among which MAs performs well and can effectively find the global optimal threshold combination.

Among the several MTIS methods, histogram-based segmentation methods are widely used. Since one-dimensional histograms cannot capture the spatial relationship between pixels, they are easily affected by noise, resulting in low segmentation accuracy. The mis-segmentation phenomenon will be quite severe if the target region occupies a small portion of the image. In 1989, Abutaleb proposed an IS method based on a 2D histogram, combining the local average pixel with the original grayscale histogram to better reflect the spatial characteristics of the image and improve the accuracy of segmentation. However, this method has high computational complexity and requires large computing resources. In addition, certain characteristics of the image, including particular points and edges, are not taken into consideration by Abutaleb’s approach for generating a 2D histogram. Consequently, a 2D histogram based on Non-local Means (NM) is suggested in order to overcome the aforementioned issues, and the Renyi entropy is employed as the fitness function of the improved method.

The flowchart of the Renyi entropy MTIS method based on ADSPAO is displayed in Figure S2. Initially, the input image is grayscale processed, and the generated grayscale image is subjected to NM denoising to generate an NM image. Then, the grayscale image and the NM image are combined to form a 2D histogram. Moreover, using ADSPAO as the search approach, and Renyi entropy is used as the fitness function to maximize the 2D Renyi entropy to determine the ideal optimal threshold combination. Finally, the grayscale image is segmented using the obtained threshold combination to obtain a segmented and color-mapped image.
Non-local means 2D histogram

The NM filtering method offered by Buades is an effective method for removing noise. This denoising technique can preserve the detailed features of the image to the maximum extent and make full use of image’s redundant information. The basic idea is to weighted average the pixels in the image that have comparable neighborhood structures in order to get an estimate of the current pixel. The calculation of the NM is as follows. Assuming that g(e) and g(f) are the grayscale values of pixels e and f in image I, then:Among them, NM(e) is the grayscale value of pixel e in the image after NM denoising, w(e, f) refers to the corresponding weights of pixels e and f, calculated as shown in Equation 26.where, U represents the normalization coefficient, h means the control parameter, v(e) and v(f) are local means, B(e) refers to the pixel block of size n × n around pixel e, and the same applies to B(f).

The NM 2D histogram is generated by the NM image and the grayscale image. If the size of the grayscale image Ig of the image I is m × n, then the size of the NM image G obtained by NM filtering is also m × n. In the 2D histogram, the point (t, s) is generated by the grayscale value of the pixel (e, f) in the grayscale image Ig and the grayscale value of the pixel (e, f) in the NM image G. Here, T(t, s) refers the number of times the point (t, s) appears, and its joint probability density is given by Equation 30. The final 2D histogram is acquired after normalization by the formula.
Renyi entropy for 2D histogram

Renyi entropy is a generalized version of Shannon entropy because it has a positive parameter α, which makes it more adaptable to many types of images in IS than Shannon entropy. Renyi entropy depends on NM images and grayscale images. Here, let the grayscale image Ig and the NM image G have grayscale levels L − 1. The pixel grayscale level s and the NM grayscale level t form an L − 1 tuple (s1, t1), (s2, t2), …, (sL−1, tL−1). Then the expression of Renyi entropy δα(t, s) is as shown in Equations 31 and 32.where, α is set to 0.5. By configuring the tuples (s1, t1), (s2, t2), …, (sL−1, tL−1), the set s1, s2, …, sL−1 that maximizes the total Renyi entropy is determined as the optimal segmentation thresholds.
Quantification and statistical analysis

All statistical analyses in this study were conducted to ensure the reliability and reproducibility of the experimental results. The quantitative performance of different algorithms was evaluated using mean (Avg) as the measure of central tendency and standard deviation (Std) as the measure of dispersion, reflecting both optimization accuracy and stability. For medical image segmentation tasks, the performance was assessed using PSNR, SSIM, and FSIM, and the corresponding average and standard deviation values were reported.

To statistically compare the performance differences between ADSPAO and other competing algorithms, both the Wilcoxon Signed-Rank Test (WSRT) and the Friedman Test (FT) were employed. The WSRT was used for pairwise nonparametric comparisons at a significance level of 0.05, while the FT was applied to rank multiple algorithms across benchmark functions. The FT rankings and WSRT win/loss/tie results are reported in the corresponding tables.

All statistical details, including numerical results, dispersion measures, and significance comparisons, are provided in the Results section, figure legends, and tables. The convergence behaviors of the algorithms are illustrated through convergence curves in the figures.
